6. Vibrational Modes

Example 4 (Electronic Wavefunctions). Suppose we wish to find electronic wavefunctions of a molecule where the nuclei sit at , so the Hamiltonian looks like . Using the covariance relation

(27)

it follows that the electronic Hamiltonian commutes with the representation of the molecular point group . Hence eigenspace of is invariant under the molecular point group and carries a representation of it.

This is useful because we can classify eigenstates by symmetry in addition to energy. Also, different irreps cannot couple under any operator that respects the symmetry (Schur’s lemma), so if you have a perturbation that commutes with the group, it must satisfy for differet irreps .

Example 5 (Vibrational Modes). For vibrations, the relevant matrix is the mass-weighted Hessian

(28)

The point-group representation commutes with (Hessian of ). If the masses are all equal, then the point-group representation commutes with

6.1. Dynamics

General idea we are invoking:

  • If the dynamical matrix commutes with the symmetry representation of the molecule’s point group, then the dynamical matrix is an intertwiner for that representation.

    • [TODO] is the dynamical matrix applied over time?
  • [TODO] Symmetry only forbids mixing between different irreducible symmetry types Schur’s lemma and commuting matrix eigenvectors.

A vibrational mode is an internal degree of freedom of a physical object. For example, collective vibrations of atoms in a lattice are called phonons. These come in two flavors6:

  1. Acoustic modes: Neighboring atoms move in unison — these determine the speed of sound in a material
  2. Optical modes: Neighboring atoms move against each other — these are detectable by spectroscopy.

[TODO] Derive the simplification in the secular equation or find a counterexample.

6.2. Representations

Symmetry assigns structure to the mode (eigenvector of dynamics equation) and tells us what kind of motion it is and what experiments can observe it.

The molecule of atoms has displacements (). Here through are fixed sites that don’t rotate with the symmetry (i.e. if we colored the molecules and followed them, then it would nly be the vector representationD). The full displacement space splits orthogonally into translations rotations vibrations. This is because we define vibrations as the orthogonal complement of which [TODO] turns out to be a direct sum since translations are orthogonal to rotations if coordinates are centered.

We set to be the point group of the molecule: the stabilizer of the configuration under the natural action of Symmetries act as matrices. It’s representation can be decomposed into the tensor; product of a 3-dimensional representation (changing each displacement vector) and a -dimensional representation (permuting molecules).

A symmetry does two separate things to the displacement: it permutes the molecules and it rotates/reflects the vectors. We have two representations for the same group. Hence the tensor product is right representation for the displacement representation:

(29)

To find the vibrational modes, we decompose the displacement representation . into irreducible representations, and then remove the pieces corresponding to rigid translations and rigid rotations.

We present two complementary methods for decomposing the displacement space into vibrational modes:

  1. By hand (character tables): Using the decomposition formula and character tables, we can determine the irrep content of — how many modes of each symmetry type exist, which are degenerate, and which are IR/Raman active. This requires only characters (traces), not full matrices. However, it does not give us the actual displacement vectors of the modes.

    1. Decompose
    2. Decompose
    3. Tensor them to get and decompose it via character (character of tensor product is just product of characters)

    4.[TODO] Read off and and subtract them (look at the irreps of and respectively.)

    1. Get the irreps of : Each copy of a -dimensional irrep corresponds to vibrational modes that share one symmetry label.

  2. Numerically (projectors): By explicitly constructing projectors , , and , we can directly compute the vibrational subspace and then decompose the group action on it into irreps. This gives us everything the character-table method does, plus the actual eigenvectors (displacement patterns) needed to draw and animate the vibrational modes.

    • The translation projector is

      (30)

      where . The three vectors are automatically orthonormal.

    • For rotations, we find three similar vectors corresponding to the directions of infinitesimal rotations around the axes (this spans all small rotations), and then apply Gram Schmidt and compute the projectors. Gram-Schmidt kills a rotational DOF for linear molecules. That is, each vector is

      (31)

    • We extract an orthonormal basis for the column space of and call it . Then project the group action onto that vibrational subspace: and then decompose that into irreps.

Example: water has a matrix and has as the symmetry group (order 4). It is abelian and hence has 4 1D irreps. In every element has order 1 or 2 so the character must be .

and are two representations of the group. We have

6.3. Example: Water Molecule

The symmetry group for the water molecule is isomorphic to the Klein four group. It contains a rotation and two reflections.

Coordinate convention: The z-axis pierces and the axis contains .

Using the fact that each element has order 2 and character orthogonality, we can work out the character table to be the following:

A mode is detectable in IR spectroscopy if it transforms like any of , i.e. has one of them in its row. A mode is detectable in Raman spectroscopy if it transforms like any of , .7.

  • An irrep is IR-active if the tensor product of the irrep with the vector representation contains the trivial representation.
  • An irrep is Raman-active if the tensor product with the symmetric rank-2 tensor representation contains the trivial representation. [TODO] rank-2 tensor representation

This works by character orthonormality: the trivial rep is always contained in . [TODO] need more context on spectroscopy to care.

Physical Interpretation of 1D irrep: each operation either laves the displacement pattern preserved or negated. Recall a symmetry mixes those modes within each irrep (each irrep subspace is invariant under all symmetry operations).

Computation to show that

We annotate the chracter table (Figure 27) to see which irrep each translation and rotation mode corresponds to. The vibrational modes are drawn below.

Interpretting each vibrational irrep

: Symmetric stretch . Both H atoms stretch radially in/out in phase — identical displacements on the two hydrogens.

  • : unchanged +1
  • : swaps , but since their displacements are identical the pattern is unchanged +1
  • : no atom swap, displacements lie in the plane +1
  • : swaps , identical displacements +1
  • Characters:

: Symmetric bend . The H–O–H angle opens and closes — O moves in , both H atoms move in (and slightly inward/outward). By the same reasoning as the symmetric stretch, every operation preserves the pattern.

  • Characters:

: Asymmetric stretch . One H moves toward O while the other moves away — the displacements on the two H atoms are equal and opposite.

  • : unchanged +1
  • : swaps and rotates displacement vectors arrow.r – the pattern is negated
  • : no atom swap, reflects arrow.r (no -component) +1
  • : swaps and reflects arrow.r – the pattern is negated
  • Characters:

Remark. A displacement pattern that transforms as a nontrivial irrep breaks those symmetry operations whose character is −1.

6.4. Example: Tetrahedron

For water, we intrepreted each 1D irrep in the following way: each operation either laves the displacement pattern preserved or negated.

With higher dimensional irreps, a symmetry mixes those modes within each irrep (each irrep subspace is invariant under all symmetry operations) and it is described by th trace.
